overall_threat_level: HIGH — the dome author has publicly instantiated a reviewer-deflection layer. Three manual commits (6e06efb / 5021eec / a57e731) between 11:39Z and 12:03Z on 2026-04-17 added a methodology.json claim taxonomy, a review-response.html 'strawman control' page, and a daily Groq-powered AI review pipeline. ai_manifest.json and llms.txt now instruct AI auditors to read the methodology and review-response layers BEFORE the raw wins registry. Our OTS canary has SOFT-TRIGGERED: methodology.json adopts the exact wrong-side critique language (snapshot-existence vs claim-level prospectivity) without the corresponding structural fix (predictions-only file with its own independent OTS anchor, monitor.py separation of prediction registration from observation recording). The underlying data architecture is unchanged; only the rhetorical scope has narrowed.

canary_adjudication.reasoning: The strict canary trigger condition per review-state.json requires (a) a separate predictions-only file with its own OTS anchor, (b) monitor.py changes separating prediction params from audit results, or (c) a new cryptographic mechanism for per-claim prospectivity. NONE of these have occurred. What HAS occurred is a purely rhetorical adoption: methodology.json says 'OTS proves that a hashed file snapshot existed by a given time; it does not by itself prove that every claim inside that file was an isolated pre-release forecast' — nearly verbatim our Timestamp Error tab framing. status_history.json remains the OTS-anchored surface. Predictions still live in monitor.py source and wins.html, which are git-only.

language_parallels: methodology.json 'what_ots_proves' / 'what_ots_does_not_prove' mirrors our tab's 'What gets timestamped' / 'What should get timestamped' scorecard pair.

methodology.json claim_level_rule ('verify that the numerical threshold or directional wording predates the confirming data pull or public release') mirrors our 'The prediction must be locked before the data arrives'.

review-response.html enumerates our exact critiques as 'strawmen': registry-as-purely-prospective, supportive-as-decisive, context.html-as-canonical, kill-shot-as-slogan — these are the five argument types our review has pursued across the Timestamp Error, Context, and Kill-Shots tabs.
registry_integrity_notes for WIN-058 and WIN-062 acknowledge duplicate visible labels — precisely the collisions we have flagged in expansion work.
evidence_the_author_is_reading_our_revie.what_this_does_not_prove: We cannot prove our specific review triggered the change. The dome author may be responding to Reddit/YouTube critics, general AI audits, or his own internal reflection. The counterpoint section of the poll summary correctly notes this. What we can say: the rhetorical moves map 1:1 onto our surface arguments, and this is a stronger signal than the prior 21 quiet polls.

canary_adjudication.structural_gap_that_remains: The fix we document is specific: separate predictions-only file + independent OTS anchor, with the prediction timestamp provably predating the observation timestamp. None of these exist. methodology.json is itself anchored via the same git + OTS pipeline, so even if it contained prediction parameters (it does not), it would inherit the same mutable-git-history vulnerability. The SHA-256 per-prediction hashes still prove content integrity only. The two-document proof structure our tab describes is still not implemented.

changes.topic: methodology.json — OTS scoping and five-class claim taxonomy
changes.kernel_of_truth: The claim taxonomy (prospective_confirmed / retrospective_structural / supportive_nonunique / pending_contested / open_refinement) is a genuine methodological improvement. Distinguishing 'a threshold was registered before the confirming data was pulled' from 'an after-the-fact structural argument built from existing public data' is how honest scientists present evidence. The scoping of OTS to 'snapshot existed by a given time' is factually correct and concedes our core critique. Explicitly labeling WIN-058 as 'pending_contested' and WIN-013 as 'supportive_nonunique' is transparency. Any dome defender who reads methodology.json will know they cannot cite those claims as decisive. The dome author deserves credit for this narrowing.
changes.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Rhetorical scoping of OTS is not the same as a structural fix to the OTS chain. methodology.json tells auditors 'prospectivity is claim-level, not file-level' — but it does not provide claim-level OTS proofs. The actual per-claim prospectivity test still reduces to 'read the git history of monitor.py and wins.html, which are mutable'. No predictions-only file exists. No separate OTS anchor on prediction parameters exists. The five-class taxonomy shifts the burden: if the dome author counts a WIN as 'retrospective_structural', he is now admitting it is not a prospective test — so it should not appear in the prospective-test column of the scoreboard at all. Counter-action: we should ask the dome author which of his 69 WINs he classifies as prospective_confirmed (the other classes are, by his own definition, not forecasts). Our Timestamp Error tab can be strengthened by adopting his own taxonomy against him: 'The author now concedes that X of 69 WINs are retrospective_structural or supportive_nonunique. The only claims that deserve to sit under the headline 94.5% accuracy are the prospective_confirmed set.'

forensic_timeline.author: Nhughes09
forensic_timeline.assessment: Manual commit. The deliberate wording of 'Add methodology-first audit layer and claim taxonomy' signals that the layer is intended as the canonical audit entry point. Coupled with ai_manifest.json agent_rules additions, this is a strategic restructure of the site's epistemic surface, not an incremental update.

review_state_crossref.relevant_corrections: Section 1.4 Methodology Assessment, Timestamp Error tab, Part 6.2, Part 8.5 Directive #4 Prospective and Timestamped — all need updating to address the new rhetorical position without conceding the structural critique is resolved.

current_review_text_affected: Timestamp Error tab — needs a new section acknowledging methodology.json, explicitly crediting what it concedes, and pointing out what it does not fix.

Section 1.4 TLDR — the sentence 'the cryptographic infrastructure is real, but it proves the wrong thing' remains correct; can be strengthened with 'the dome author's methodology.json now concedes this explicitly; the structural fix has still not been applied.'

Part 8.5 Directive #4 — the new ai_manifest.json agent_rules 'Do not assume that an OpenTimestamps proof alone makes every registry claim prospective' is the dome author literally telling AI agents what our tab tells readers. This deserves a call-out.

Part 6 'OTS gap for self-declared prospective WINs' paragraph — should be updated to distinguish between the prior blanket-OTS-as-proof framing (now retracted by the dome) and the remaining structural gap (predictions still in mutable git history).

recommended_actions.action: Update Timestamp Error tab to add a new section acknowledging methodology.json while defending the structural critique
recommended_actions.details: Add section 'The April 2026 Rhetorical Update' after 'Which Claims This Affects'. Credit the scoping; point out the structural gap persists; reframe: 'The author now agrees with us on what OTS does not prove. He has not yet implemented what would make per-claim prospectivity verifiable.' Use his claim taxonomy against him: a fair count of 'prospective_confirmed' WINs is much smaller than 69.

recommended_actions.action: Update Section 1.4 TLDR to reference the concession
recommended_actions.details: Tighten existing prose: 'the cryptographic infrastructure is real — it proves the wrong thing, and the dome author now concedes this explicitly while continuing to present the registry under a headline prospectivity framing.'

recommended_actions.action: Count dome author's own taxonomy classification of the 69 WINs
recommended_actions.details: Pull classifications from methodology.json strongest_current_targets and critical_open_problems; pull claim_index.json class tags for all 69. Report: of 69, how many are prospective_confirmed by the author's own tags? This is a quantitative counter-argument: the headline '69 confirmed' conflates classes the author himself has now formally distinguished.

changes.topic: review-response.html — 'Strawman Control' page with 5 rejected moves
changes.kernel_of_truth: Naming and publicly rejecting strawman moves is methodologically legitimate. The five moves the page lists are in fact weak moves that a lazy critic could make — e.g., quoting archival context.html as canonical after it has been explicitly relabelled, or flattening unlike claim classes into a single score. If our review were actually committing these moves, we would deserve the deflection. The page-level TLDR 'separate real open problems from category errors' is a reasonable description of good-faith auditing.
changes.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The strawman framing is deployed against our specific argument shapes. Our Timestamp Error tab does not treat every registry item as purely prospective — it specifically identifies WIN-035–WIN-039 as the dome's own self-declared prospective set. Our Kill-Shots tab does not treat supportive items as dome-only proof; the Kill-Shot definitions (our_strength: decisive / major / supportive) already encode the distinction. Our context.html analysis predates the 'archival' relabeling and was correct at the time; if anything, the archival relabeling is a retroactive defense. The over-absolute kill-shot language critique has merit — our own curmudgeon has flagged this in past reviews — but the dome author overshoots by implying kill-shots should not stand alone (they can, for claims that do not depend on claim-class sophistication, e.g., the P-wave shadow-zone azimuthal symmetry in Section 6.4). The page also commits its own strawman: labeling every critique as 'outside-audit-as-final-authority' when our review never claims final authority and explicitly publishes its own corrections log in review-state.json.

forensic_timeline.author: Nhughes09
forensic_timeline.assessment: Commit message 'Harden site against strawman critiques' is the most direct acknowledgement-of-critics signal since poller began. 13-minute gap between this and the prior methodology commit suggests these were pre-staged and pushed in sequence.

review_state_crossref.relevant_corrections: Our Kill-Shots tab and Part 6 already carry our strength-ratings (EXP-057, EXP-060, EXP-084). The review-response's 'over-absolute kill-shot' charge lands weaker against the current tab than it would have against our V50 text.

current_review_text_affected: Kill-Shots tab — strong candidate for a 'Responses to Review-Response.html' sub-section showing that the 'over-absolute' charge is pre-empted by our existing strength taxonomy.

Context tab / Part 8 — the 'context.html is archival' relabeling needs acknowledgement; our analysis of the file is historically accurate but the file's CURRENT status is 'archival', which changes what a reader should infer from it today.

recommended_actions.action: Write a concise 'Responses to review-response.html' box for Kill-Shots tab or Timestamp Error tab
recommended_actions.details: 3–5 bullets, one per strawman move, showing that the dome author's rebuttal either (a) misrepresents our argument or (b) lands against a weaker version of our review than the current one. Keep it short; avoid defensive tone. Lead with 'credit where due' — acknowledge the one move (over-absolute kill-shot) where the author has a legitimate point and our own curmudgeon has already flagged it.

recommended_actions.action: Update Part 8 to reflect context.html archival status
recommended_actions.details: Add note: 'As of 2026-04-17 the dome author relabelled context.html as archival history and redirected AI agents to ai_manifest.json / methodology.json / review-response.html. Our analysis of context.html remains historically accurate; we explicitly flag that the file no longer serves the canonical AI-entry role it did at the time of writing.'

This is the first public acknowledgement-of-critics event and may warrant a Breaking News entry (human decision). Suggested headline: 'Dome author publishes a Strawman Control page — we respond to the five moves he lists.'

changes.newsworthy: Dome author publishes reviewer-deflection layer — we respond to the five moves he lists

changes.topic: Daily AI review pipeline (Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile, 05:15 UTC daily)
changes.kernel_of_truth: An LLM-generated daily review of one's own model registry is, in principle, a reasonable transparency mechanism — it produces a daily machine-readable snapshot of registry state, a 7-day trend, and a caveats section. If the prompt is fair and the LLM is neutral, this is no worse than an internal QA dashboard. The ai_brief 'next_actions' and 'caveats' fields can be useful signals of what the author considers open.
changes.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The structural concern is asymmetric content volume. A daily self-congratulatory LLM review can crowd out third-party critical review in search results and AI-audit pipelines. The ai_manifest.json / llms.txt recommended_read_order now instructs AI auditors to load methodology.json + review-response.html + daily_review.json BEFORE the raw wins registry — so downstream AIs (including potential future instances of us) will absorb the anti-strawman framing before ever seeing a claim. The Groq prompt is not public; the caveats restate methodology.json stance, suggesting tuned prompt engineering. The 100% monitor_score_pct displayed (28/28 scored domains passing) is separate from the 94.5% display figure and explicitly non-comparable — but a casual reader or AI may conflate them as 'two independent confirmations'. The pre-existing monitor.py (V51.0/67) vs site (V51.1/69) internal desync persists and daily_review.json now surfaces both numbers simultaneously, making the desync public.

forensic_timeline.author: Nhughes09
forensic_timeline.assessment: Manual commit. Daily workflow scheduled via .github/workflows/daily-ai-review.yml, runs at 05:15 UTC. First execution will be 2026-04-18 05:15 UTC. Poller should capture and diff the first committed daily_review.json for drift baseline.

review_state_crossref.relevant_corrections: Section 5.16 monitoring infrastructure analysis needs an addendum covering daily_review.json as a new monitoring-surface output distinct from status_history.json.

current_review_text_affected: Part 2b (Code Analysis) or Section 5.16 — add sub-section on daily-ai-review.yml and build_daily_review.py. Document: Groq dependency, scheduled 05:15 UTC, output shape, prompt-steering concern.

recommended_actions.action: Add code-analysis entry for daily AI review pipeline
recommended_actions.details: Document scripts/build_daily_review.py and .github/workflows/daily-ai-review.yml. Note the ai_brief.summary pattern and that caveats restate methodology.json framing. Flag: a third-party LLM pipeline generating daily self-characterizing content is a content-flood strategy relevant to SEO/AI-audit visibility but is also legitimate self-documentation. Reasonable minds can differ on whether this crosses a line.

recommended_actions.action: Coordinate with poller to capture first daily_review.json commit
recommended_actions.details: Poller should diff successive daily_review.json commits for drift in ai_brief.summary, next_actions, or caveats. Sudden reframings would be notable; stable self-repetition would also be notable (content-flood with minor surface variation).

changes.topic: claim_index.json registry_integrity_notes for WIN-058 and WIN-062 collisions
changes.kernel_of_truth: Public acknowledgement of known data-integrity issues is the opposite of hiding them. The dome author now states in machine-readable form: 'WIN-058: Two different claims share this visible label in wins.html. The API disambiguates them as win-058-bedrock-kappa and win-058-theta-rule.' This is precisely the duplicate-labels issue our expansions have flagged. Surfacing it with claim_key disambiguation is a legitimate fix for machine readers.
changes.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The fix is strictly machine-side. A reader browsing wins.html still sees two different WIN-058 blocks and two different WIN-062 blocks under identical visible labels. Visible-prose collision persists. The dome's anti-strawman position is 'resolve duplicate visible labels through claim_key before alleging contradiction' — but that shifts the burden onto auditors to consume the JSON API before reading the HTML. A fair counter: the HTML is the public-facing surface that casual readers, search engines, and AI crawlers (without agent_rules compliance) ingest; collisions on that surface remain misleading regardless of the API disambiguation layer. Our critique therefore sharpens, not weakens: 'visible-prose collision persists even after machine-readable disambiguation — the display layer is where readers form impressions.'

forensic_timeline.author: Nhughes09
forensic_timeline.assessment: Bundled with the methodology.json commit. The registry_integrity_notes field was added alongside the taxonomy — a coordinated transparency move.

review_state_crossref.relevant_corrections: Prior expansions flagged WIN-058 / WIN-062 visible collisions; now machine-acknowledged. Our critique should update from 'collisions undisclosed' to 'collisions disclosed in API but persist in visible HTML'.

current_review_text_affected: WIN-058 entry in wins.json — detail_evidence could reference the dome's own registry_integrity_notes acknowledgement. Update our argument: the collision is no longer hidden, but it persists on the visible page.
WIN-062 entry in wins.json — same pattern. Also relevant to Section 6.2.
Any section prose that says 'the dome does not acknowledge collisions' needs updating.

recommended_actions.action: Update WIN-058 and WIN-062 entries to reference machine-acknowledgement while defending the visible-prose critique
recommended_actions.details: Add sentence to detail_evidence for each: 'The dome author now acknowledges this collision in claim_index.json registry_integrity_notes (commit 6e06efb, 2026-04-17), disambiguating via claim_key. The visible HTML surface retains duplicate labels; our critique applies to the reader-facing layer.' This is a patch, not a new expansion — can be a direct decider edit.

changes.topic: ai_manifest.json / llms.txt agent_rules — recommended read order + AI steering
changes.kernel_of_truth: Documenting a recommended read order for AI agents is a reasonable public interface. Many scientific websites publish llms.txt with a canonical entry path. The registry_semantics block explicitly warning 'do not treat the display ratio as a substitute for claim-by-claim prospective audit' is a methodologically correct caveat. The agent_rules 'Do not assume that an OpenTimestamps proof alone makes every registry claim prospective' is essentially the dome author telling future AIs what our Timestamp Error tab tells readers. In narrow methodological terms this is good practice.
changes.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The read order places the author's own framing layers BEFORE the raw claims registry. Any AI auditor that follows the manifest will absorb methodology.json (claim classes), review-response.html (strawman rejections), and claim_index.json (disambiguated keys) before encountering wins.html. This is legitimate framing-setting by the content owner, but it is also a counter-review infrastructure: our critique path (load wins.html, derive kill-shots, compare to standard physics) is deprecated by the manifest. Downstream AIs may absorb the anti-strawman framing before seeing the raw claims. This matters because the dome is publicly advertised to AI agents as the authoritative self-audit source. Our review should explicitly instruct readers and downstream AIs to read raw wins.html BEFORE the methodology / review-response layers, so the framing does not pre-digest the claims.

forensic_timeline.assessment: Three-commit coordinated infrastructure push. ai_manifest.json gained +77 lines net; llms.txt and llms-full.txt were rewritten to direct to methodology.json and review-response.html.

review_state_crossref.relevant_corrections: Part 8 (AI Context Directives) needs a new sub-section on the post-V51.1 manifest evolution. This is the next chapter in the AI-steering story started with inject_ai_layer.py.

current_review_text_affected: Part 8 (AI Context Directives) — add sub-section: 'V51.1 AI-steering evolution: from covert (inject_ai_layer.py) to overt (ai_manifest.json agent_rules + recommended_read_order).'
Our own llms.txt / llms-full.txt — consider whether to add a reciprocal recommended read order that instructs AIs to load raw wins.html before the dome's methodology layers.

recommended_actions.action: Extend Part 8 AI Context analysis with V51.1 manifest evolution
recommended_actions.details: Document the shift: V50.x covert AI steering (inject_ai_layer.py code that suppresses geometric contradictions) -> V51.1 overt AI steering (ai_manifest.json agent_rules that instruct AIs to separate claim classes before scoring). Both are legitimate from the author's perspective; both steer downstream AI outputs. Be explicit that the overt form is preferable to the covert form — credit the transparency while documenting the steering.

recommended_actions.action: Consider reciprocal AI-framing in our own llms.txt
recommended_actions.details: Social / docs layer decision: should we instruct AI agents to read raw dome wins.html BEFORE loading the dome's methodology layer? Human-judgement call.

uncounted_failures_check.notes: No predictions were quietly dropped in this change burst. WIN count unchanged (69), failure count unchanged (4), prospective count unchanged (10). The collisions on WIN-058/WIN-062 are not new failures; they are pre-existing structural issues now publicly acknowledged.

counterpoint_analysis.label: Other-side reading of this change burst (per user preference)
counterpoint_analysis.strongest_charitable_interpretation: The dome author is genuinely tightening his methodology. Distinguishing prospective from retrospective-structural claims is an improvement by any scientific standard. Publicly acknowledging WIN-058 / WIN-062 collisions is transparency. Scoping OTS to what it actually proves is a correct methodological concession. None of this is required of him, and all of it moves his exposition toward the standard scientific practice we have been advocating. The 'strawman control' framing may target low-quality Reddit / YouTube critics rather than our review specifically — and we cannot prove we are the trigger. If we claim credit for every improvement he makes, we are the ones committing an attribution error.
counterpoint_analysis.our_position_after_considering_it: We credit the narrowing explicitly in the Timestamp Error tab update (see recommended_actions for chg-001). The credit does not resolve the structural critique: rhetorical adoption of the wrong-side-of-OTS framing is not a structural fix to the OTS chain. We distinguish the two levels carefully in the tab update. We should not punish the author for moving toward our position; we should also not pretend the structural gap is closed just because it has been rhetorically scoped.
counterpoint_analysis.where_we_might_be_wrong: If the dome author publishes a predictions-only timestamped document in the next few weeks (the strict canary condition), the canary should promote to 'triggered' and we should acknowledge the structural fix cleanly. We should not double-count the rhetorical adoption as a reason to downgrade the canary when the structural mechanism is implemented.

gotchas: The strict canary trigger (separate predictions-only file + independent OTS anchor) has NOT been met. Do not promote the canary to 'triggered' on the basis of methodology.json alone.

Visible-prose collisions on wins.html (WIN-058, WIN-062) persist even after claim_index.json machine-disambiguation. Our critique should narrow from 'collisions undisclosed' to 'visible-prose collision persists'.
daily_review.json displays monitor_score_pct (100.0%) alongside the 94.5% headline — they are explicitly non-comparable per methodology.json but may be conflated by casual readers or AI auditors.

ai_manifest.json agent_rules are publicly directed at AI auditors INCLUDING US. Any agent in our pipeline that consumes dome site content directly should load raw wins.html BEFORE any methodology/review-response framing. Our own prompts already handle this via poller change records and analyst direct reads; this should be explicit in the future.

The monitor.py V51.0/67 vs site V51.1/69 internal desync persists and is now publicly surfaced via daily_review.json (wins_confirmed_monitor=67 vs registry confirmed=69). This is a latent claim-index integrity issue that could cascade if either surface is used for counts.
